data.title: Key facts: Bloom Energy's stock surges; Tradr launches leveraged ETFs
data.description:
• Bloom Energy's stock has risen fivefold this year, driven by demand for on-site power, particularly from data centers. Partnerships with firms like Oracle enhance its fuel cell deployment.1
  • Tradr has launched leveraged ETFs that include Bloom Energy Corp, alongside companies like Celestica, NANO Nuclear, and Synopsys.2

data.title: Key facts: BlackRock nears $26.8B ACS deal; exceeds voting rights limits
data.description:
• BlackRock's Global Infrastructure Partners is nearing a $26.8 billion deal with Spain's ACS to develop data centers, acquiring a 50% stake in ACS's Digital & Energy unit.12
  • On November 12, 2025, BlackRock, Inc. exceeded a regulatory limit for voting rights in IP Group PLC. It also crossed a similar threshold for Helios Towers PLC on November 11, 2025.3
  • BlackRock has partnered with RIQ to develop reinsurance solutions for clean energy projects, enhancing coverage and capital efficiency in the global energy transition.4

data.title: Key facts: Beyond Meat cuts 64 jobs; reports 13% retail volume drop; Mizuho lowers price target
data.description:
• Beyond Meat has cut its workforce by about 44 employees in 2025 and suspended operations in China, resulting in an extra reduction of 20 employees.1
  • Beyond Meat's third-quarter report showed a 13% decline in U.S. retail volumes and a 27% drop in foodservice volumes, accompanied by a $77 million impairment charge.2
  • Mizuho has reduced its price target for Beyond Meat (BYND) from $1.50 per share to $1.00 per share while maintaining its rating of 'Underperform'.3

data.title: Key facts: B of A Raises Trane Technologies Price Target to $550; Climate Week NYC 2025 Panel on Decarbonization
data.description:
• B of A Securities has increased the price target for Trane Technologies to $550.00 per share, up from the previous target of $490.00.1
  • Trane Technologies hosted a panel during Climate Week NYC 2025, focusing on decarbonization in the built environment, emphasizing collaboration and innovation for sustainability goals.2

data.title: Key facts: Apple partners with Tencent; invests in Greater Bay Area; exec sells $1M in shares
data.description:
• Apple has struck a deal with Tencent, earning a 15% commission on WeChat mini-games and apps, down from 30%. This opens a new revenue stream for Apple.12
  • Apple continues to invest significantly in China, particularly in the Guangdong-Hong Kong-Macao Greater Bay Area, despite geopolitical tensions, due to the region's unique supplier concentration.3
  • Apple Inc (AAPL) officer Chris Kondo sold 3,752 shares worth about $1 million on November 12, 2025, reducing his holdings to 15,098 shares.4

data.title: Key facts: Aflac CFO Max increases shares; Check for Cancer initiative launched
data.description:
• On November 12, 2025, Aflac CFO Broden Max acquired 4,668 shares, increasing his total to 135,360 shares, including 135,021 directly and 339 indirectly.1
  • Aflac's Check for Cancer initiative began in Boston at the Head of the Charles® Regatta, targeting a 10% increase in cancer screenings over the next decade.2

data.title: Why option traders think Dec. 10 will be the most important day for markets before the end of 2025
data.description:
By Joseph Adinolfi

S&P 500 options are pricing in a 1.3% swing that day - the largest daily implied volatility before the new year

Option traders are pricing in a big swing in the S&P 500 on Dec. 10.

On Dec. 10, investors will have to contend with a double helping of event risk: The November consumer-price index report is scheduled to be released that morning, while the Federal Reserve will announce whether or not it has decided to cut interest rates again in the afternoon.

So, options traders are bracing for volatility - with premiums on option contracts tied to the S&P 500 SPX due to expire that day pricing in a 1.3% swing. That is the biggest implied move expected for the rest of 2025, according to a team of equity-derivative strategists at BNP Paribas.

Due to the longest government shutdown in U.S. history, it has been months since investors received key data reports on the state of the U.S. labor market. Economists at BNP Paribas estimated that long-delayed September jobs data could be released within days.

On Thursday, White House economic advisor Kevin Hassett said the October nonfarm-payrolls report could also be released soon, although the data likely will not include a reading on the unemployment rate.

A team of analysts at Citigroup said they expected the November jobs report to be released with only a slight delay. But it is possible investors might not receive another full report on the labor market before the Fed makes its decision on interest rates next month, the BNP team noted.

Comments from senior Fed officials pushing back on the likelihood of a rate cut next month have inspired investors to dial back expectations. Data from CME Group put the likelihood of another rate cut next month at just under 50% on Thursday.

During an interview with MarketWatch published on Thursday, Cleveland Fed President Beth Hammack said driving inflation back to the Fed's target of 2% is critical for the central bank's credibility. The Cleveland Fed's NowCast inflation model puts the year-over-year inflation rate at around 3%.

The latest official U.S. reading, for the month of September, also put the rate at 3%. The CPI report for October has been delayed due to the recently resolved government shutdown.

-Joseph Adinolfi

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Lamar Advertising Is Maintained at Buy by Citigroup
data.description: (20:07 GMT) Lamar Advertising Price Target Raised to $145.00/Share From $135.00 by Citigroup

data.title: Canada Pledges Support to Blackstone-Backed LNG Facility — Market Talk
data.description: Canada unveils another tranche of infrastructure and resource projects that will get government support to clear remaining regulatory and financing details. Among the bigger projects is the proposed Ksi Lisims LNG export facility. The project, backed in part by Blackstone Energy Transition Partners, was initially approved by Canadian authorities in September. PM Mark Carney says the project would become Canada's second-largest LNG facility, and is expected to attract C$30 billion in investment. Canada pledges to help Ksi Lisims with financing, and coordinate regulatory approval for a gas pipeline and electricity-transmission line that links to the LNG export facility. (paul.vieira@wsj.com; @paulvieira)

data.title: PROJECTS: Carbon Clean steps up Middle East push with modular carbon capture technology
data.description:
Anoop Menon

UK-based Carbon Clean, a global carbon capture technology company, is stepping up engagement in the Middle East as it seeks to expand deployment of its modular CycloneCC system across the region’s hard-to-abate sectors.

The company, which completed a pilot with Adnoc and is now testing with Aramco, is talking to players in oil and gas, steel, cement, and other energy-intensive industries in order to integrate its modular carbon capture technology into their decarbonisation roadmaps, Chief Technology Officer Prateek Bumb told Zawya Projects.

“Large scale carbon capture projects are expensive, need extensive planning, and often face cost overruns due to their size,” he explained during an interview at ADIPEC 2025. “Our CycloneCC solution offers a modular, scalable pathway for companies to reduce [carbon capture] costs and achieve decarbonisation targets.”

Bumb, who co-founded Carbon Clean in 2009 with Chair and CEO Aniruddha Sharma, said space limitations at industrial sites make large carbon capture facilities difficult to deploy, positioning compact modular technologies like CycloneCC as a practical alternative.

He noted that CycloneCC is well-suited even for large Carbon Capture and Storage (CCS) hubs, which aggregate CO2 from industrial clusters and share transport and storage infrastructure to cut costs and achieve economies of scale.

“You want to capture CO2 more efficiently and cost-effectively — or in some cases, industries simply lack the space. That’s where our technology fits in, capturing CO2 and feeding it into the hub’s transport and storage network.”

Prateek Bumb, Chief Technology Officer, Carbon Clean

He clarified that Carbon Clean focuses solely on carbon capture, not storage or utilisation.

“Our role is to reduce carbon capture costs so that utilisation or storage become economical and then, we scale this modular system.”

The company has already scaled CycloneCC to 100,000 tonnes per annum capacity, with plans to reach 500,000 tonnes per annum within three years.

“So, if a customer wants to capture a million tonnes of CO2, just two to three trains would be needed,” he said.

Compact technology, proven chemistry

At the heart of CycloneCC is rotating packed bed technology, which evolved from Carbon Clean’s proprietary Amine-Promoted Buffer Salt (APBS) solvent chemistry – it is deployed at nearly 50 sites worldwide and credited with capturing 2.8 million tonnes of CO2 over the past decade.

While the solvent chemistry reduced operating costs for carbon capture plants, early deployments revealed a recurring challenge - limited space at customer sites.

“We achieved 30 to 35 percent operating cost reduction with solvent innovation,” Bumb said. “But when deploying conventional plants, customers were struggling because of lack of space. We wanted to de-risk that challenge, and that was the genesis of rotating packed bed technology.”

Other challenges included high construction costs and system engineering and integration constraints.

Bumb said CycloneCC is a prefabricated system that can be deployed in six to eight months compared with three to four years for traditional systems.

“Compressing that timeline is one of the factors in reducing the costs significantly,” he explained. “With CycloneCC, we reduced capital costs by 20–25 percent compared with conventional systems. Our goal is to halve total costs in the next three years, making carbon capture cheaper and downstream utilisation and storage economically viable for industrial users.”

But he cautioned that the cost of deployment and achievable cost reductions vary, influenced by factors such as site characteristics, geographic location, and in recent times, tariff structures.

“We’re on track to achieve the cost-reduction targets we set during our 2021 fundraising,” Bumb said. “Through the Adnoc deployment, we met all agreed KPIs, demonstrating both modularity and scalability. The project achieved Technology Readiness Level 7 (TRL 7).”

Potential sectors

Sectors with the greatest potential for CycloneCC include in the region cement, waste-to-energy, shipping, biomass, oil and gas, noted Bumb

“These are some of the key industrial sectors that we are actively involved in,” he said, adding that beyond regulatory mandates, many Middle Eastern industrial companies are voluntarily adopting carbon capture as part of their sustainability strategies.

On the utilisation front, regional clients are considering methanol, sustainable aviation fuel and building materials use cases to de-risk scale-ups.

The Carbon Clean executive said that companies have started viewing CO2 as a valuable molecule, and are working on business models to monetise it.

“Early movers will get the advantage in terms of deployment going forward.”

Policy and validation

Bumb emphasised that global and regional policy frameworks remain critical to accelerate commercial adoption, calling for financial incentives, tax credits, and government-backed pilots to de-risk early-stage projects.

He also cited Europe’s Northern Lights CCS initiative in Norway -  the world’s first open-access CO2 transport and storage project - as a model of coordinated infrastructure and business alignment.

“If the right business model is in place to make carbon capture industry profitable, things will really fly,” he said.

Carbon Clean primarily operates under a technology licensing and equipment sales model.

“We are seeing a lot of interest on carbon capture as a service model,” Bumb noted.

Validation from national oil and gas giants like Adnoc and Aramco has helped accelerate customer conversations in the region, he said

“When an Aramco or an Adnoc selects us for pilot or demonstration, it gives us lot of credibility,” he explained. “They follow extremely rigorous technical and commercial evaluation processes before investing in any pilot, which validates the viability and scalability of our technology. Our experience working with oil majors is that they want to really see technically verified on their facility.”

He added that such validation also helps accelerate decision-making with industrial players globally.

(Reporting by Anoop Menon; Editing by SA Kader)

(anoop.menon@lseg.com)

Subscribe to our Projects' PULSE newsletter that brings you trustworthy news, updates and insights on project activities, developments, and partnerships across sectors in the Middle East and Africa.

data.title: The board of directors of LIDDS AB intends to propose a reduction of the company’s share capital for loss coverage
data.description:
GOTHENBURG, SWEDEN - The board of directors of LIDDS AB (publ) (“LIDDS” or the “Company”) intends to propose that an extraordinary general meeting of the Company resolve on a reduction of the Company’s share capital in order to cover incurred losses. The purpose is to restore the Company’s equity in relation to the registered share capital and thereby remedy capital deficiency.

As of the date of this press release, the Company’s registered share capital amounts to SEK 7,232,556. According to the interim report for the second quarter of 2025, published on 28 August 2025 and available on the Company’s website, the Company’s equity as of 30 June 2025 amounted to approximately SEK 2.071 million. Through the proposed reduction of share capital for loss coverage, the board intends to remedy the capital deficiency so that, after registration of the reduction, the Company has full coverage for its share capital. The reduction of share capital for loss coverage entails no repayment to shareholders and does not affect the number of shares in the Company; that is, the reduction is proposed to be carried out without cancellation of shares.

The board’s proposal forms part of the effort to appropriately safeguard the values that the Company and the group control and thereby, in the long term, create the highest possible shareholder value. The Company primarily controls the following values: the NanoZolid technology platform and associated patent; the subsidiary Noviga with the drug candidate NOV202 and associated patent; the Company’s listing on Nasdaq First North Growth Market; and potential value in accumulated tax loss carryforwards.

The proposal is submitted in light of the Company’s obligation to hold a second control meeting within eight months of the first control meeting on 28 May 2025, at which a new balance sheet for liquidation purposes must be presented. If, at the second control meeting, the balance sheet for liquidation purposes does not show that equity amounts to at least the registered share capital, the Company is obliged to enter into liquidation.

The reduction of share capital for loss coverage is subject to a resolution at an extraordinary general meeting of the Company, which is planned to be held on 18 December 2025. A separate notice convening the extraordinary general meeting will be published by way of a separate press release.

data.title: Key facts: C3.ai stock drops 5.3%; Ehikian appointed as new leader
data.description:
• C3.ai's stock fell 5.3% as investors took profits from high-valuation growth stocks following a strong AI rally and the end of a government shutdown, which will release delayed economic reports.1
  • C3.ai appointed Ehikian as leader, known for scaling AI software firms and federal tech modernization, aligning with the company's growth areas.2

  • Bitfarms has over $1 billion in liquidity and is receiving strong political support, positioning the company for continued expansion and a planned redomicile in the U.S. in 2026.3

  • Bank of America (BAC) saw a stock rise during a financial sector rally, driven by optimism over a possible US government shutdown resolution, alongside a notable increase in the Dow Jones.2
  • Bank of America reached record highs on Wednesday as the Dow Jones Industrial Average surged over 300 points, closing above 48,000 for the first time.3

  • Argus Research has lowered the price target for Ameriprise Financial (AMP) to $554.00 per share, a decrease from the previous target of $568.00.2

data.title: Key facts: American Airlines shares rise 3.9%; recovery plans announced
data.description:
• American Airlines' shares rose 3.9% as investors favored value stocks amid worries about high tech valuations and a potential end to a long government shutdown.1
  • American Airlines announced its strong positioning for a swift recovery following the federal government's reopening, crediting its operational decisions that focus on minimizing disruption.2
  • American Airlines has entered into a note purchase agreement, as disclosed in a recent SEC filing.3

data.title: Key facts: Amazon's $38B OpenAI deal boosts profits; secures NFL streaming rights
data.description:
• Amazon has secured a $38 billion deal with OpenAI, contributing to a broader trend of increased demand for cloud services, which is leading to a surge in data center construction.1
  • Amazon posted strong quarterly profits, boosted by a $9.5 billion pretax gain from its AI investment in Anthropic, whose valuation surged from $61 billion to $183 billion.2
  • Amazon.com has secured the streaming rights for Thursday night NFL games, reinforcing its position in the live sports streaming market amid a decline in traditional cable subscriptions.3

data.title: Key facts: Alibaba faces food delivery price war; invests in AI and rebrands app
data.description:
• Alibaba (BABA) is in a price war in food delivery with JD.com and Meituan, offering major discounts to boost market share, raising concerns about profit margins and profitability.12
  • Alibaba plans to invest 15% of its revenue in AI and cloud infrastructure over the next three years. CEO Eddie Wu aims to position the company as a top global AI service provider.3
  • Alibaba is rebranding its mobile AI app from Tongyi to Qwen, enhancing AI for better shopping on Taobao. The app will launch on iOS and Android, with a global version planned.45

  • TD Cowen has increased the price target for Akamai Technologies to $104.00 per share, up from the previous target of $98.00.2

data.title: Key facts: Airbnb Q3 2025 revenue reaches $4.10B; sues Biloxi; rural Spain growth
data.description:
• Airbnb's Q3 2025 earnings showed revenue of $4.10 billion, exceeding forecasts with a 9.7% year-on-year growth. Nights and experiences booked reached 133.6 million, up by 10.8 million.1
  • Airbnb has sued Biloxi, Mississippi, claiming the city and the hotel association conspired to restrict short-term rentals, violating antitrust laws and inflating hotel prices.2
  • Airbnb notes rural Spain is less popular than similar areas in France, Britain, and Italy, but anticipates increased interest from the 2026 solar eclipse and government rural tourism efforts.3

data.title: Key facts: AMD shares rise 9%; forecasts 80% chip sales growth; partners with OpenAI and Oracle
data.description:
• AMD shares surged 9% after its analyst day, forecasting 80% annual chip sales growth and a $1 trillion data-center market by 2030, with 60% revenue growth expected.123
  • AMD is partnering with OpenAI and Oracle, expected to boost revenue. The company will launch MI400 chips and Helios rack system in 2026 to enhance its AI computing market position.4

data.title: Diversified Energy Is Maintained at Buy by Citigroup
Canada names proposed Ksi Lisims LNG project for fast-tracking
data.description:
Key points:
  • Ksi Lisims LNG capacity planned at 12 million tonnes annually
  • Shell and Total Energies have signed 20-year LNG purchase agreements
  • Company CEO says final investment decision expected in early 2026

By Amanda Stephenson

Canada said on Thursday it will add the proposed Ksi Lisims LNG facility to its major projects list for fast-tracking, a designation the company behind the project said makes it more likely to proceed.

Prime Minister Mark Carney made the announcement in Prince Rupert, British Columbia, where the facility would be located with direct shipping access to Asia. Ksi Lisims has a planned capacity of 12 million metric tons per year, which would make it Canada's second-largest liquefied natural gas export terminal after Shell-led LNG Canada, which began operations this year.

Carney is aiming to speed up natural resource project construction to boost the economy that has been damaged by U.S. tariffs.

The government created this year a major projects office tasked with streamlining approvals and helping to coordinate financing for large-scale infrastructure projects deemed in the national interest.

INVESTMENT DECISION EXPECTED NEXT YEAR

The proponents of Ksi Lisims — Houston-based Western LNG; a consortium of Canadian natural gas producers called Rockies LNG; and the Nisga'a First Nation, who own the land for the project — have not made a final investment decision to proceed.

But Western LNG CEO Davis Thames told Reuters inclusion on Canada's major projects list brings Ksi Lisims closer to fruition, adding the proponents expect to decide whether to proceed early next year.

"It's pretty obvious that the different constituents of the project will see this (designation) as being favorable," Thames said, adding expedited permitting times would decrease the likelihood of construction delays once Ksi Lisims is under way.

"Time is money, and when you have to stop construction because you didn't get a permit in time, that's what just kills budgets," he said.

Shell and TotalEnergies have signed 20-year LNG purchase agreements with Ksi Lisims. The company is working to finalize other commercial agreements with LNG purchasers before committing to a final investment decision, Thames said.

Construction is already under way on the 900-km (560-mile) Prince Rupert Gas Transmission pipeline, which will transport natural gas from northeast B.C. to Ksi Lisims and was also on the government's list.

Canada's major projects office has also been tasked with collaborating with affected Indigenous groups to help fast-track infrastructure development.

Having the backing of the major projects office increases the odds of the project going ahead, but is not a guarantee that a positive final investment decision will be reached, said RBN Energy analyst Martin King.

"Locking up the needed financing is also something that needs to be finalized," King said.

Carney's government said fast-tracking the latest round of projects will spur private investment worth C$56 billion ($39.93 billion). Other projects announced on Thursday for inclusion on the fast-track list include the North Coast Transmission line - an estimated C$6 billion electrification project by BC Hydro that is expected to provide clean energy to the province's LNG and mining industries - and the Crawford Nickel project, an open-pit nickel and cobalt mine in Ontario by Canada Nickel Company.

($1 = 1.4024 Canadian dollars)

data.title: Judge allows lawsuit over Trump's anti-DEI grant policy despite Supreme Court ruling
data.description:
By Nate Raymond

Eight Democratic-led states can pursue a lawsuit challenging a U.S. Department of Education policy that led to the cancellations of millions of dollars in teacher-training grants despite a U.S. Supreme Court decision that allowed President Donald Trump's administration to terminate the funding, a federal judge ruled on Thursday.

The Trump administration had sought to dismiss the case in light of the U.S. Supreme Court's April decision, arguing it belonged instead in the U.S. Court of Federal Claims, which handles monetary disputes with the government.

U.S. District Judge Angel Kelley in Boston in her ruling agreed that the Supreme Court's recent holdings in Trump-era cases meant she could not force the administration to reinstate grants it terminated as part of its effort to nix diversity, equity and inclusion initiatives.

But she said the states could still pursue claims in her court that the Department of Education overstepped its legal authority. If the states win, organizations that received grants that were terminated could file a new case before the Court of Federal Claims seeking money damages, Kelley said.

"When all is said and done, grants to fund certain educational programs were terminated," wrote Kelley, an appointee of Democratic President Joe Biden. "Today's holding indicates this Court lacks any power to bring these grants back. However, Plaintiffs are not without remedy."

A spokesperson for California Attorney General Rob Bonta, who is leading the litigation, said his office was pleased Kelley agreed she could hear the states' challenge to policies that resulted in "vital" grants being canceled.

"We look forward to continuing to make our case to her in court," the spokesperson said in a statement.

The Education Department did not respond to a request for comment.

The $65 million in K-12 teacher preparation grants at issue were among more than $600 million that the Education Department said it canceled in February because they were used to train teachers and education agencies on "divisive ideologies," including DEI.

Then-Acting Secretary Denise Carter had issued a directive days earlier stating the Education Department must ensure its grants do not support programs or organizations that take part in DEI initiatives.

The states sued, arguing the administration's actions were unlawful and would exacerbate a teacher shortage by effectively eliminating the Teacher Quality Partnership and Supporting Effective Educator Development grant programs.

U.S. District Judge Myong Joun, a Biden appointee, issued a temporary restraining order in March requiring the grants to be reinstated, but the 6-3 conservative majority Supreme Court put that order on hold in April.

On remand, the case was reassigned to Kelley, who on Thursday said the Supreme Court's four-paragraph ruling had "confused" the lower courts because it seemingly contradicted a 1988 decision by the high court.

She said the Supreme Court "somewhat abated" the confusion in August, when the justices in a different case allowed the administration to proceed with cuts to National Institutes of Health grants for research it deemed to support DEI and LGBTQ health care.

The deciding vote in that case was conservative Justice Amy Coney Barrett, who in a concurring opinion endorsed a two-track form of litigation that would allow a litigant to challenge an agency policy's in district court and pursue retrospective claims for money in the Court of Federal Claims.

Kelley relied on Barrett's opinion for Thursday's ruling, saying the "two-track litigation framework preserves binding authority and upholds the systemic stability intended by the doctrine of stare decisis."

The case is State of California et al v. Department of Education, U.S. District Court for the District of Massachusetts, No. 1:25-cv-10548.

For California: Laura Faer and Alexis Piazza of the California Department of Justice

For the federal government: Michael Fitzgerald of the U.S. Attorney's Office for the District of Massachusetts

Read more:

US Supreme Court lets Trump cut diversity-related NIH grants

US Supreme Court lets Trump cut teacher training grants in DEI-related case

US judge orders Trump administration to restore teacher preparation grants

data.title: Fitch Assigns CVC Credit Capital Solutions III's Subscription Facility Obligations at 'A+'/Stable
data.description:
Fitch Ratings-London-13 November 2025:

Fitch Ratings has assigned an 'A+' rating to CVC Credit Capital Solutions III (Vehicle) LLC (and affiliated entities, collectively, the fund)'s revolving subscription facility. The Outlook is Stable.

The sole borrowers under the subscription facility are CVC Credit Capital Solutions III (Vehicle) SCSp and CVC Credit Capital Solutions III (Vehicle) LLC. CVC Credit Capital Solutions III (Offshore D) SCSp and CVC Credit Capital Solutions III (Offshore E) SCSp act as facility guarantors. The facility is secured by the uncalled capital commitments (UCC) of the guarantors' limited partners (LPs). Each guarantor jointly and severally guarantees the facility obligations, cross collateralising the guarantors' LP pools.

The fund was launched in 2022 and is managed by CVC Credit Partners Group, a subsidiary of CVC Capital Partners plc (CVC).

Fitch's rating only addresses the credit risk of the subscription facility and should not be viewed as an indicator of the fund's past or future performance, or suitability as an investment. The rating only applies to the participations of Barclays Bank Ireland PLC and ING Bank N.V. London Branch.

KEY RATING DRIVERS

Limited Partner Pool Somewhat Concentrated: The investor base consists of 47 LPs with a Herfindahl-Hirschman Index (HHI) of 987 and a corresponding effective LP count of 10, which Fitch regards as somewhat concentrated. Our view of diversification is driven by the HHI and the effective LP count. The largest LP types in the pool are investment funds (37.4%), asset managers/banks (20.2%) and sovereign wealth funds (16.8%), primarily domiciled in Europe (46.2%), North America (16.8%), and Asia-Pacific (16.8%). Fitch applied its tier classification framework to derive rating assumptions for unrated investors, which represent 50.5% of the analysed LP pool.

The effective LP count represents the number of equally weighted LPs, based on UCC, needed to attain the same HHI.

Reduced Resilience to Stress Scenarios: The baseline Quantitative Rating Indication (QRI) is 'AA-', driven by the quality and somewhat concentrated nature of the LP pool, the implied 72.0% effective maximum advance rate, and an assumed 18-month maximum exposure period, which is informed by the presence of an 18-month pay down requirement in the facility agreement. Fitch's QRI-sensitivity analysis indicates sensitivity to certain stresses, including increased correlations, negative changes in the LP pool and stressed recoveries, which led to a two-notch downward adjustment to the baseline QRI, resulting in a final QRI of 'A'.

Qualitative Assessment: Fitch's Qualitative Assessment (QA) score of '2' is supported by the manager's strong resources, franchise, and economic incentives for investors to fund future capital calls given the level of capital called to date, as well as the terms of the limited partnership agreement. The QA is constrained by the fairly limited performance record of the fund series and FX risk. The positive QA score led to a positive one notch adjustment to the final QRI.

Rating Cap: The facility's rating is capped at 'AA+' as more than 20% of the LP base exposure is to unrated investors, although it not currently applicable.

INVESTMENT MANAGER

As of 30 June 2025, CVC had approximately EUR200 billion of assets under management, across seven investment strategies, including private equity, secondaries, credit and infrastructure. CVC was established when the management team of Citigroup subsidiary, Citicorp Venture Capital, was rebranded and spun-out in 1993. Citicorp Venture Capital was established as a subsidiary of Citigroup in 1981.

RATING SENSITIVITIES Factors that Could, Individually or Collectively, Lead to Negative Rating Action/Downgrade

Deterioration in the diversification and/or credit profile of the LP pool that results in a sustained lower baseline and/or adjusted QRI than the QRIs relied on at the time of the most recent rating action

Sustained deterioration of fund performance and/or underlying investment value

A material deterioration in CVC's investment and operational capabilities

Factors that Could, Individually or Collectively, Lead to Positive Rating Action/Upgrade

Improvement in the diversification and/or credit profile of the LP pool of the fund that results in a sustained higher baseline and/or adjusted QRI than the QRIs relied on at the time of the most recent rating action

Additional capital called and sustained positive fund performance may result in a positive change to the QA

Increase in the share of the explicitly rated underlying LP pool to above 80% would result in the removal of the 'AA+' rating cap and may make the rating eligible for an upgrade . However, Fitch views this as unlikely given the current low exposure to explicitly rated LPs

REFERENCES FOR SUBSTANTIALLY MATERIAL SOURCE CITED AS KEY DRIVER OF RATING The principal sources of information used in the analysis are described in the Applicable Criteria.
